title: Line Cook
metaTitle: Line Cook Resume: Tips & Examples for 2026
metaDescription: Build a standout line cook resume with real examples, key skills, and ATS keywords. Get hired faster with actionable resume advice.
h1: How to Write a Line Cook Resume That Gets Results
intro: Your line cook resume needs to show speed, precision, and consistency under pressure—not just a list of stations you've worked. We'll walk you through the exact format, skills, and bullets that hiring managers and restaurant operators actually look for.
audience: Entry-level and mid-career line cooks looking to land roles at casual dining, fine dining, or fast-casual restaurants; career switchers from food service or culinary training programs.

topSkills.name: Prep and Plating
topSkills.why: Chefs need confidence that you can maintain consistent food quality and presentation under lunch or dinner rush.

topSkills.name: Line Station Mastery
topSkills.why: Hiring managers want to see you've worked specific stations (sauté, grill, fryer, cold prep) because deep knowledge = speed and fewer mistakes.

topSkills.name: Food Safety & Sanitation
topSkills.why: Health code compliance and proper handling are non-negotiable; restaurants can't afford violations or foodborne illness incidents.

topSkills.name: Kitchen Equipment Operation
topSkills.why: Competence with commercial ovens, ranges, fryers, slicers, and other gear shows you won't slow down the line.

topSkills.name: Time Management Under Pressure
topSkills.why: Service windows are tight; chefs want cooks who can execute multiple orders in parallel without sacrificing quality.

topSkills.name: Team Communication
topSkills.why: A busy line depends on clear call-outs, feedback, and coordination with expo, other cooks, and dishwashing.

topSkills.name: Menu Knowledge
topSkills.why: Understanding ingredients, preparation methods, and dietary restrictions helps you work smarter and answer customer questions.

topSkills.name: Kitchen Inventory & Cost Control
topSkills.why: Minimizing waste, proper portioning, and respecting par levels directly impact restaurant margins.

sampleBullets.weak: Prepared food and worked at multiple stations during busy shifts.
sampleBullets.strong: Executed 80–120 covers per shift across sauté and grill stations, maintaining 98% order accuracy and plating consistency during peak hours.
sampleBullets.lesson: Specific numbers (covers, accuracy %, station names) prove you can handle real volume and show measurable outcomes.

sampleBullets.weak: Followed food safety rules and kept the station clean.
sampleBullets.strong: Maintained HACCP compliance and passed all health inspections with zero violations; deep-cleaned fryer and station equipment daily.
sampleBullets.lesson: Naming compliance standards (HACCP) and zero violations makes safety expertise concrete, not vague—restaurants care about audit readiness.

sampleBullets.weak: Helped train new staff on kitchen procedures.
sampleBullets.strong: Trained 5+ new line cooks on cold prep station procedures and knife techniques, reducing rework and training time by 40%.
sampleBullets.lesson: Quantifying people trained and the impact (time saved, error reduction) elevates mentoring from a side task to a leadership strength.

commonMistakes.mistake: Listing stations without context or outcomes.
commonMistakes.fix: Pair each station with a metric (covers handled, speed improvement, quality score) or specific dish/technique responsibility.

commonMistakes.mistake: Omitting food safety certifications or kitchen software.
commonMistakes.fix: Add certifications (ServSafe, HACCP) and any POS, inventory, or ordering system experience in a dedicated section or resume summary.
Be specific: sauté, grill, fryer, pastry, charcuterie, sauce prep—chefs want to know your actual strengths, not guesses.
If you reduced ticket time, cut waste, or improved consistency, say it with numbers—restaurants live on efficiency.

commonMistakes.mistake: No mention of menu knowledge or ingredient sourcing.
commonMistakes.fix: Reference any experience with seasonal menus, local suppliers, dietary accommodations, or special diets (vegan, keto, allergies).

structureTips: Lead with a brief professional summary (2–3 lines) that highlights your strongest station(s) and any certifications—this catches the eye immediately.
Use a 'Core Competencies' or 'Key Skills' section to list stations, equipment, cuisines, and safety credentials; it mirrors how restaurants post job descriptions and helps ATS matching.
In your work history, organize bullets by station or achievement type (speed, consistency, training, innovation) rather than pure chronology—this mirrors how kitchens evaluate applicants.
Place food safety, ServSafe, or culinary education prominently; add any specialty training (butchery, pastry, molecular gastronomy) in a separate line if it's relevant to your target roles.
salarySignal: Entry-level US line cook salaries typically range from $26,000 to $32,000 annually, with experienced cooks and those in major metros or fine dining reaching $35,000–$45,000+.

faq.q: Should I list every station I've worked, or just my best ones?
faq.a: Lead with your strongest 2–3 stations; list others briefly in a skills section. Chefs hire based on depth first, breadth second. If you're applying to a specific role (e.g., pastry cook), emphasize that expertise.

faq.q: How do I show I work well under pressure without sounding overworked?
faq.a: Use hard numbers: 'Executed 100+ orders per shift with zero remakes' or 'Maintained plating standards during 200+ cover service.' This shows capability and consistency, not burnout.

faq.q: Do I need formal culinary training to land a line cook job?
faq.a: No. Many successful line cooks start with on-the-job training or community college programs. Highlight any certifications, culinary school, or structured apprenticeships you have, but prioritize demonstrable experience and food safety certs (ServSafe, HACCP).

faq.q: What if I have gaps in my kitchen work history?
faq.a: Be honest and brief. If you took time off, note it simply ('2022–2023: career break'); if you switched to a different field, highlight any transferable skills (time management, customer service). Restaurants care about reliability—showing you're aware of gaps builds trust.

faq.q: How important is culinary innovation or menu development on a line cook resume?
faq.a: Nice-to-have, not essential. Focus first on execution, consistency, and teamwork. If you helped develop a signature dish, tested new techniques, or led a menu refresh, mention it, but only after you've proven core line skills.
